unit_name: Mathematical Models and Numerical Methods with First Order DEs
hook: You already know how to solve differential equations analytically — but most real-world problems, from predicting population collapse to modeling drug dosages, don't come with clean closed-form solutions. This unit bridges the gap between pure theory and messy reality by building models from physical laws and developing powerful numerical tools when exact answers are out of reach. How does a cup of coffee cool, a population explode, or a pollutant spread — and what do we do when the math fights back?
concept: Every topic here uses first-order differential equations as a language to describe change in the real world, then asks: can we solve it exactly, and if not, how do we approximate it reliably? This is the practice of mathematical modeling paired with numerical analysis.

topic_roadmap.description: You will build and solve exponential and logistic growth models to predict how populations — and radioactive substances — change over time.
Apply first-order DEs to track how a substance flows through tanks or compartments, setting up rate-in minus rate-out equations from physical descriptions.
Model the temperature of an object over time using a proportionality law, and solve for quantities like time of death or the temperature of a cooling cup of coffee.
Master the foundational numerical algorithm for approximating solutions to DEs by stepping forward with tangent-line estimates when exact solutions are unavailable.

Upgrade your numerical toolkit by learning higher-accuracy algorithms — the Improved Euler (Heun) method and the classic RK4 — that dramatically reduce approximation error with smarter slope estimates.
Quantify how and why numerical errors accumulate by distinguishing local truncation error from global error, and understand how step size controls the reliability of your approximations.
